1: lōʾ lānû yhwh lōʾ lānû kî-lĕšimĕkā tēn kābôd — "not to us, O LORD, not to us — but to your name give glory" — the double lōʾ lānû (not to us... not to us) is emphatic self-abnegation; the psalm opens by disavowing any claim to divine credit. The glory belongs to the divine name, grounded in two covenant attributes: ʿal-ḥasdĕkā ʿal-ʾămittĕkā — "for your steadfast love (ḥesed) and your faithfulness (ʾĕmet)" — the two pillars of covenantal divine character (Exodus 34:6).
2: lāmmāh yōʾmĕrû haggôyim ʾayyēh-nāʾ ʾĕlōhêhem — "why should the nations say, 'Where then is their God?'" — the taunt may reflect military defeat or the challenge of Babylonian exile. The nations' challenge is theological: a God who cannot be seen in a cult statue seems powerless by ancient Near Eastern standards. The psalm's response (vv3-8) is that YHWH's invisibility is not weakness but transcendence — and that visible idols are themselves the problem.
3: wĕʾĕlōhênû baššāmayim kōl ʾăšer-ḥāpēṣ ʿāśāh — "but our God is in the heavens; all that he pleases he has done" — the contrast is absolute: the nations ask "Where?" and the answer is "in the heavens" — beyond all spatial localization. kōl ʾăšer-ḥāpēṣ ʿāśāh (all that he pleases he has done) — divine sovereignty over all events, contrasted with the impotence of idols that cannot even see or speak.
4: ʿăṣabbêhem kesef wĕzāhāb maʿăśēh yĕdê ʾādām — "their idols are silver and gold — the work of human hands" — ʿăṣabbîm (idols, from ʿāṣab, to fashion/shape; also related to grief/pain) — the word choice for "idols" carries a derogatory connotation. The critique "work of human hands" is the core of the OT idol polemic: the material precedes the maker. Compare Isaiah 44:9-20 (the craftsman who burns half a tree for warmth and makes an idol with the other half) and Jeremiah 10:1-16.
5: peh-lāhem wĕlōʾ yĕdabbērû ʿênayim lāhem wĕlōʾ yirʾû — "they have mouths but do not speak; eyes they have but do not see" — the systematic dismemberment of the idol's sense organs (vv5-7) proceeds through mouth, eyes, ears, nose, hands, feet, and throat. The irony is double: the idol has the form of human sense organs that YHWH gave to humans, but the form is hollow — none of them function. The idol is a parody of the human being made in God's image, possessing the form but not the nĕšāmāh (breath of life).
6: ʾoznayim lāhem wĕlōʾ yišmāʿû ʾap lāhem wĕlōʾ yĕrîḥûn — "ears they have but do not hear; a nose they have but do not smell" — the idol's nose is functionless in both biological and cultic terms: it cannot smell the sacrifices offered to it. The contrast with YHWH is implicit: YHWH smells Noah's sacrifice (Genesis 8:21: wayyāraḥ yhwh ʾet-rêaḥ hannîḥōaḥ). The idol's organ exists but cannot engage with what is brought to it.
7: yĕdêhem wĕlōʾ yĕmîšûn raglêhem wĕlōʾ yĕhallēkû lōʾ-yehgû bigrônām — "their hands but they cannot feel; their feet but they cannot walk; they do not make a sound from their throat" — māšaš (to feel, grope) — the hands lack tactile sensation. hāgāh (to mutter, meditate, make a sound) — used of the growl of a lion, the mourning of a dove, the murmur of Torah meditation (Psalms 1:2). The idol's throat produces nothing — no growl, no word, no breath. The polemic ends with this most basic absence.
8: kĕmôhem yihyû ʿōśêhem kōl ʾăšer-bōṭēaḥ bāhem — "like them will be those who make them — all who trust in them" — the idol polemic reaches its theological apex: worshippers become what they worship. If the idol is a non-seeing, non-hearing, non-breathing thing, the worshipper who trusts in it becomes correspondingly deaf, blind, and spiritually lifeless. This principle of worship-as-formation runs counter to the fear of the LORD in Ps 111-112, where the one who fears YHWH takes on YHWH's own character.
9: yiśrāʾēl bĕṭaḥ bayhwāh ʿezrām ûmāginnām hûʾ — "Israel, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield" — the sudden shift from description to direct address (v9) is a liturgical pivot, probably call-and-response between cantor and congregation. The triple refrain (vv9/10/11) addresses Israel, the house of Aaron, and those who fear the LORD — the three groups present in the temple court. ʿezer (help) and māgēn (shield) are the two primary metaphors for divine protection: active assistance and defensive covering.
10: bêt ʾahărōn bĕṭĕḥû bayhwāh ʿezrām ûmāginnām hûʾ — "house of Aaron, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield" — the bêt ʾahărōn (house of Aaron) = the priestly families. Their trust is placed in the same source as all Israel: the LORD, not their priestly status or ritual expertise. The identical refrain drives home that there is one source of help and protection for all.
11: yirʾê yhwh bĕṭĕḥû bayhwāh ʿezrām ûmāginnām hûʾ — "those who fear the LORD, trust in the LORD! He is their help and their shield" — the third group, yirʾê yhwh (those who fear the LORD), may refer to Gentile proselytes or God-fearers in the temple court (cf. Acts 10:2, Psalms 13:16), expanding the circle beyond ethnic Israel. Together the triple refrain says: whatever your status, your help is not in idols but in the living God.
12: yhwh zĕkhārānû yĕbārēk — "the LORD has remembered us; he will bless" — zĕkhārānû (he has remembered us) — divine remembering always entails divine acting; it is not a cognitive event but a covenant event. The past tense grounds the future blessing: because he has already remembered, the threefold blessing that follows is certain. The triple yĕbārēk (he will bless) echoes the triple refrain of vv9-11.
13: yĕbārēk yirʾê yhwh haqqĕṭannîm ʿim-haggĕdōlîm — "he will bless those who fear the LORD, the small together with the great" — the pairing haqqĕṭannîm ʿim-haggĕdōlîm (the small together with the great) emphasizes that the blessing is not status-based. The fear of the LORD is democratizing: it places the small and the great on the same footing before the divine patron.
14: yōsēp yhwh ʿălêkem ʿălêkem wĕʿal-bĕnêkem — "may the LORD increase you — you and your children" — yāsap (to add, increase) — the blessing is multigenerational increase, harking back to the Abrahamic promise (pārāh wĕrābāh, be fruitful and multiply, Genesis 1:28; Psalms 9:1). The Hiphil jussive: "may he cause you to increase."
15: bĕrûkhîm ʾattem layhwāh ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ — "blessed are you by/from the LORD, who made heaven and earth" — the divine title ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth) appears also in Psalms 121:2 and Psalms 124:8. The idol critique (vv4-8) makes this doxology sharper: unlike idols made by human hands, Israel is blessed by the one who made all that is.
16: haššāmayim šāmayim layhwāh wĕhāʾāreṣ nātan libnê-ʾādām — "the heavens — heavens — belong to the LORD, but the earth he has given to the children of man" — the repeated haššāmayim šāmayim is emphatic: the upper realm is reserved for YHWH alone. But the earth is given (nātan, perfect tense — permanent grant) to bĕnê-ʾādām (children of Adam/humanity). This vertical division undergirds v17: the dead in Sheol are in a third space — neither YHWH's heaven nor humanity's proper earth.
17: lōʾ hāmētîm yĕhalĕlû-yāh wĕlōʾ kol-yōrĕdê dûmāh — "the dead do not praise the LORD, nor any who go down to silence" — dûmāh (silence, the realm of the dead) — Sheol as the land of silence. This is one of the most direct OT statements about the condition of the dead: they cannot praise. The same conviction appears in Psalms 6:5 ("in death there is no remembrance of you; in Sheol who will give you thanks?") and Psalms 88:10-12. This is not a denial of afterlife per se but an affirmation that Sheol is outside the covenant space of praise. Hebrews 2:12 (citing Psalms 22:22) presents Christ's resurrection as precisely what reverses this: the risen Christ praises in the assembly, opening what Sheol had closed.
18: waʾănaḥnû nĕbārēk yāh mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām hallĕlûyāh — "but we — we will bless the LORD from this moment and forever. Praise the LORD!" — waʾănaḥnû (but we) is emphatic: in contrast to the dead who cannot praise, the living community takes up the obligation. The formula mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām echoes Psalms 113:2; the Egyptian Hallel frames time itself as praise-time. The closing hallĕlûyāh returns to the beginning of Ps 113, closing the liturgical bracket of the first Hallel pair.
